quiz.title: Unit 9 Quiz 9, Biology I
quiz.subtitle: Genetics: Inheritance Patterns, Blood Groups, and Test Crosses
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Recessive Inheritance, Universal Donor, Test Cross, Genotype Determination, Phenotypic Ratios

questions.question: Attached earlobes in humans is inherited condition. The allele for attached earlobes is recessive. What are the chances of parents, both having attached earlobes, to have a child with attached earlobes?
questions.explanation: If both parents have attached earlobes and the trait is recessive, both parents must be homozygous recessive (ee). ee × ee produces all ee offspring, so 100% chance of child with attached earlobes.

questions.question: Which type of blood group is called a 'universal donor'?
questions.explanation: Blood group O is called the universal donor because it lacks A and B antigens on red blood cells, so it can be given to patients with any blood type without causing an immune reaction. Type AB is the universal recipient.

questions.question: Albinism is a recessive trait (a). If two parents with normal pigmentation both carry the recessive gene (heterozygous), what is the probability they will have a child with albinism?
questions.explanation: Both parents are heterozygous (Aa). Aa × Aa produces offspring with genotypic ratio 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa. Albinism (aa) appears in 1/4 or 25% of offspring.

questions.question: The ability to roll one's tongue is a dominant trait (R). If a father cannot roll his tongue and a mother is homozygous dominant for tongue rolling, what are the chances their child will be a tongue-roller?
questions.explanation: Father cannot roll tongue → homozygous recessive (rr). Mother is homozygous dominant (RR). RR × rr produces all Rr offspring. All children will be heterozygous and able to roll their tongues (dominant trait expressed). Probability = 100%.

questions.question: Widow's peak is a dominant trait (W). If a man with a widow's peak (whose mother did not have one) marries a woman with no widow's peak, what is the chance their child will have a widow's peak?
questions.explanation: The man has a widow's peak (dominant), but his mother did not (ww). Therefore, the man must be heterozygous (Ww) because he inherited w from his mother. The woman has no widow's peak, so she is ww. Cross Ww × ww produces 50% Ww (widow's peak) and 50% ww (no widow's peak). Probability = 50%.

questions.question: Cystic Fibrosis is an inherited recessive condition. If both parents are healthy but are 'carriers' (heterozygous), what are the chances of them having a child who is also a healthy carrier?
questions.explanation: Both parents are heterozygous (Aa). Aa × Aa produces offspring with genotypic ratio 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa. Healthy carriers are heterozygous (Aa), which represents 2 out of 4 or 50% of offspring. AA (25%) are healthy non-carriers, aa (25%) have cystic fibrosis.

questions.question: In pea plants, tallness (T) is dominant over shortness (t). If two short pea plants are crossed, what percentage of the offspring will be tall?
questions.explanation: Short pea plants are homozygous recessive (tt). Two short plants crossed: tt × tt produces all tt offspring. All offspring will be short. 0% will be tall.

questions.question: Freckles are a dominant trait (F). If a mother has no freckles and a father has freckles (heterozygous), what is the probability that their child will NOT have freckles?
questions.explanation: Mother has no freckles → homozygous recessive (ff). Father has freckles and is heterozygous (Ff). Cross ff × Ff produces 50% Ff (freckles) and 50% ff (no freckles). Probability child will NOT have freckles = 50%.

questions.question: Huntington's Disease is caused by a dominant allele (H). If one parent has the disease (heterozygous) and the other parent is healthy (homozygous recessive), what are the chances of their child inheriting the disease?
questions.explanation: One parent has Huntington's and is heterozygous (Hh). The other parent is healthy (hh). Cross Hh × hh produces 50% Hh (affected) and 50% hh (healthy). Probability of child inheriting the disease = 50%.

questions.question: Having a 'hitchhiker's thumb' is a recessive trait (h). If a child has a hitchhiker's thumb, but both parents have straight thumbs, what must be the genotype of the parents?
questions.explanation: The child has hitchhiker's thumb → homozygous recessive (hh). Both parents have straight thumbs (dominant phenotype) but must carry the recessive allele to produce an hh child. Therefore, both parents must be heterozygous (Hh). Hh × Hh produces 25% hh offspring.

questions.question: In certain flowers, Red (R) is dominant to white (r). If a homozygous red flower is crossed with a white flower, what percentage of the offspring will be white?
questions.explanation: Homozygous red flower = RR. White flower = rr. Cross RR × rr produces all Rr offspring. All offspring will be red (heterozygous, dominant phenotype). 0% will be white.

questions.question: Cleft chin is a dominant trait (C). If two parents who are both heterozygous for a cleft chin have a child, what is the probability the child will also have a cleft chin?
questions.explanation: Both parents are heterozygous (Cc). Cc × Cc produces offspring with genotypic ratio 1 CC : 2 Cc : 1 cc. Cleft chin (dominant phenotype) appears in CC and Cc genotypes, which represent 3 out of 4 offspring. Probability = 75%.

questions.question: A pure axial flower plant (AA) is crossed with a terminal flower plant (aa). In the F₂ generation, what is the ratio of plants with axial flowers to plants with terminal flowers?
questions.explanation: AA × aa produces all Aa in F₁ (all axial flowers). F₁ self-cross (Aa × Aa) gives phenotypic ratio 3 axial (AA and Aa) : 1 terminal (aa) in F₂. Therefore, axial : terminal = 3 : 1. Option C is correct.

questions.question: Which of the following cross can be used to determine the genotype of a plant with dominant phenotype?
questions.explanation: A test cross (crossing with homozygous recessive, rr) reveals the genotype of a dominant phenotype plant. If the plant is RR, all offspring will be Rr (dominant phenotype). If the plant is Rr, offspring will be 1:1 Rr (dominant) : rr (recessive). The other crosses don't distinguish between RR and Rr.

questions.question: Which of the following crosses will give smooth and wrinkled seeds in same proportion?
questions.explanation: Rr × rr produces 1:1 ratio of Rr (smooth) to rr (wrinkled). RR × rr gives all Rr (all smooth). RR × Rr gives all smooth (1:1 genotypic but all dominant phenotype). rr × rr gives all wrinkled.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered test crosses and blood group genetics!
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review recessive inheritance and universal donor concepts.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on test cross applications and phenotypic ratios.
scoreMessages.0: Please review homozygous recessive crosses, blood group compatibility, and genotype determination.
